title: The Ramsey Numbers – New Results and New Perspectives
speaker: Julian Sahasrabudhe
abstract: In this talk I will try to motivate the interest and some of the mystery in the Ramsey numbers R(k), which are fundamental quantities in combinatorics. I will go on to discuss some recent progress on our understanding of these numbers and make some connections to problems about the geometry of random variables in high dimensions.

proof_stages.label: Quantitative formulation of diagonal Ramsey numbers
proof_stages.summary: The speaker motivates Ramsey theory through two-colour clique questions, defines R(k) as the least n forcing a monochromatic K_k, and frames the main problem as determining its asymptotic growth.

proof_stages.label: Classical greedy upper bound and its inefficiency
proof_stages.summary: The Erdos-Szekeres halving argument gives R(k) at most about 4^k, and later discussion identifies its wastefulness as paying for alternating colour choices rather than forcing many same-colour steps.

proof_stages.label: Lower-bound and random-model landscape
proof_stages.summary: Simple constructions and especially Erdos's random-colouring argument show exponential lower bounds, making random colourings the key benchmark for interpreting possible values between roughly 2^{k/2} and 4^k.

proof_stages.label: Previous two-colour breakthrough and its defects
proof_stages.summary: After surveying refinements of the classical upper bound, the speaker recalls the 2023 two-colour exponential improvement and explains why its lack of a multicolour extension, weak geometric visibility, and detour through off-diagonal estimates were unsatisfactory.

proof_stages.label: New multicolour exponential improvement
proof_stages.summary: The new theorem states that for each fixed number r of colours there is a positive delta_r giving an exponential improvement over the classical r^{rk} diagonal upper bound, and the approach also treats off-diagonal versions more naturally.

proof_stages.label: Book strategy with simultaneous reservoirs
proof_stages.summary: The proof is reframed in terms of building red and blue books, maintaining separate reservoirs and edge-density conditions so that a long run of same-colour steps can be exploited.

proof_stages.label: Geometric encoding of density loss
proof_stages.summary: Potential failures of the book step are encoded by assigning to each reservoir vertex paired high-dimensional vectors measuring red and blue neighbourhood intersections, so bad density drops become negative inner-product correlations.

proof_stages.label: Geometric dichotomy: book step or density increment
proof_stages.summary: A new geometric lemma guarantees either enough non-negative correlation to take a book step or enough clustering to obtain a density increment, yielding the mechanism that generalizes to multiple colours.

examples.name: The six-vertex monochromatic triangle problem
examples.note: The proof that every two-colouring of K_6 contains a monochromatic triangle, together with the five-vertex sharpness colouring, is used as the concrete source of the pigeonhole-halving idea later generalized to Erdos-Szekeres.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker proves the six-vertex claim by choosing a vertex and using pigeonhole, then checks a five-vertex coloring showing sharpness

examples.name: Random two-colouring benchmark
examples.note: A random red-blue colouring of K_n gives the probabilistic-method lower bound and establishes the random-graph benchmark against which possible asymptotic worlds are compared.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker runs the random coloring argument, computes the union-bound probability for monochromatic k-sets, and derives the exponential lower bound

passes.reason: The G(n,1/2) colouring at its clique threshold is a critical benchmark and supplies avoiding colorings below R(k).
A random two-colouring is used as an adversarial lower-bound construction showing that below about 2^{k/2} one can avoid monochromatic K_k.
The iid random two-colouring is used at its critical random threshold as a lower-bound obstruction to forcing a monochromatic Kk earlier.

examples.name: Simplex-like clustering of vectors
examples.note: Clustering vectors near simplex vertices explains why the geometric obstruction should lead not merely to failure of a book step but to a density-increment alternative.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes simplex-like clustering of vectors via inner products and explains how it yields the alternative density-increment behavior in the geometric lemma
